Q1: I tried to organize my study routine around my four online courses, which was honestly overwhelming at times. I'm a visual learner, so I'd create color-coded schedules and diagrams to map out what needed to be done each week. I aimed for about 24 hours of studying weekly, spread across different subjects, but honestly, my attendance in live sessions was pretty inconsistent because I was juggling extracurriculars too. I'd usually block out mornings for watching video lectures and evenings for assignments since that's when I focus best.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, studying at the same times every day, but then a big assignment would come up and everything would shift. My extracurricular activities sometimes threw off my rhythm completely. I tried to be regular, but with high stress levels, I found myself cramming more than I'd like to admit. The pressure of multiple online courses made it hard to stick to any perfect schedule.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something due or when I got a notification about new content. I also logged in pretty frequently just to check discussion boards because I found peer interactions really helpful for understanding material. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what my classmates were posting or if anyone responded to my comments. Honestly, the social aspect kept me coming back almost as much as the academic requirements did.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS actually became part of my morning routine after a few weeks. I'd wake up, grab coffee, and scroll through the course updates like I was checking social media. The discussion forums were what really hooked me into making it a habit because I genuinely enjoyed connecting with other students. It felt less isolating when I could see that other people were struggling with the same concepts or had insights to share.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me as a visual learner—I watched over 285 minutes of them and would often pause to sketch out diagrams. The discussion forums were also incredibly valuable because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click in ways the textbook couldn't. I also found the peer interactions really supportive, especially when I was confused about something. Having 42 peer interactions throughout the course kept me feeling connected and motivated even when the material got tough.
Q6: I'll admit I sometimes skipped the longer reading materials because I retained information better from videos and visual content. Some of the supplementary PDFs just sat there unopened because I felt overwhelmed managing four courses at once. I also didn't engage much with practice quizzes that weren't graded because with my stress levels, I prioritized what directly impacted my grade. It wasn't that these resources weren't valuable—I just had to make choices about where to spend my limited energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams, or when it helped me understand something I was genuinely confused about. I also valued activities that let me interact with peers because those discussions often clarified things better than studying alone. Visual content like diagrams, videos, and infographics always felt worthwhile because that's how my brain processes information best. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd struggle to prioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford with four courses demanding my attention. Required activities had this weight to them—I'd complete them no matter what because my assignment completion rate was something I took pride in. Optional stuff, even if interesting, often got pushed aside when stress was high. But sometimes the optional discussion posts were actually more engaging because there was no pressure, just genuine curiosity and conversation with classmates.
Q9: Absolutely, I did so much studying offline that never showed up in the system. I'd download lecture slides and annotate them by hand because writing things out helps me remember visually. I also used YouTube videos and external websites when the course materials didn't explain something clearly enough for me. My notebook is filled with hand-drawn diagrams and concept maps that represent hours of work the LMS would never capture.
Q10: There were many times I'd print out materials and study at a café without any internet, working through problems on paper. I also formed a small study group with classmates where we'd meet in person and work through difficult concepts together. Those peer study sessions were some of my most productive learning moments, but they wouldn't show any LMS activity. I'd also re-watch downloaded videos on my tablet during my commute, which the system wouldn't track.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my preferred way of studying. I'd access a video lecture once, download it or take detailed visual notes, and then review those materials repeatedly offline. My internet access is decent but I found it less distracting to study without being connected. Some of my best exam prep happened with printed materials spread across my desk, completely disconnected from the LMS. The initial access was just the starting point of my actual learning process.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement became really hard, especially during midterms when all four of my courses had overlapping deadlines. My stress levels were already high, and those crunch periods pushed me to my limits. I also had some personal stuff going on that affected my mental state and made it hard to focus. My attendance dropped during those times because I just couldn't handle being present for everything while trying to complete assignments.
Q13: Definitely—workload and fatigue were constant battles for me this semester. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd still log into the LMS but my engagement was more superficial, just checking boxes rather than deeply learning. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars sometimes meant I'd rush through course materials instead of taking my time. The confusion from managing four different courses also made me feel scattered, and I'd sometimes open the wrong course materials by mistake.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, stressed about other deadlines or just mentally exhausted. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what people were saying. Sometimes I'd open readings and stare at the screen but nothing was actually registering because my anxiety was too high. Those moments felt frustrating because I was technically 'studying' but I knew I wasn't actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I definitely postponed exam studying even though I knew how important it was—my exam score shows that pretty clearly. I think I avoided it because the pressure felt overwhelming and I'd convince myself I'd understand it better 'tomorrow.' With my low resilience, facing difficult material sometimes felt too daunting, so I'd do easier tasks first as a way to feel productive without tackling the hard stuff. It's something I really need to work on because it hurt my final grade significantly.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd become incredibly active in the days leading up to due dates, completing assignments and checking discussion requirements. My task completion rate was high because I never missed deadlines, but the quality of my engagement varied depending on how much time I had. Sometimes I wish I'd spread my work out more evenly instead of this deadline-driven cycle that added to my stress.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more and made me feel like my efforts were worthwhile. But critical feedback sometimes made me anxious and I'd avoid looking at it right away because I was scared of what it might say. When I did process feedback constructively, it helped me understand what to focus on for future assignments. The peer support intervention I received actually helped me handle feedback better by talking it through with classmates.
Q18: The course structure with four online courses running simultaneously was honestly challenging to manage. Having clear weekly modules helped me know what to expect, but the sheer volume of content across all courses was overwhelming. I appreciated when instructors posted materials early so I could plan ahead. The structure of discussion requirements actually worked well for me because it forced me to engage with peers, which I found valuable.
Q19: Visual content and clear organization made engagement much easier for me. When materials were text-heavy without diagrams or videos, I struggled to stay focused and had to create my own visual aids. The discussion forums were designed in a way that encouraged peer interaction, which really helped my learning. However, having multiple courses with different structures and expectations made it harder to keep track of everything and increased my stress levels.
Q20: I can actually relate to this pattern somewhat. Sometimes you're exploring, trying to understand, clicking through everything but feeling too overwhelmed or confused to actually produce work. High activity might mean someone is struggling and searching desperately for clarity rather than confidently completing tasks. It could also be that they're engaged with the learning but anxious about submitting work that might not be good enough. The activity shows effort, but effort doesn't always translate to output when stress is high.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much learning happens outside the LMS. Someone might download everything once, study intensively offline, and come back just to submit assignments. Or they might have strong background knowledge and not need to engage as much with the materials. I think my situation is kind of the opposite—high engagement but lower exam performance because I struggled with test-taking despite understanding concepts through discussion and assignments.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when you're most active, how often you engage with peers, and whether you're completing tasks on time. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or the depth of understanding you're developing. My high discussion participation shows I was engaged, but it doesn't show the stress and anxiety I was experiencing throughout.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing requirements. It's about curiosity, asking questions, connecting with peers, and putting in effort even when it's hard. For me, engagement also means finding ways to make the content meaningful and applicable to my life. It's both a mental and emotional investment in the learning process, which is why my stress levels affected my engagement quality even when my activity levels stayed high.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally while maintaining high engagement, or how the peer support intervention genuinely helped me cope with stress. It wouldn't capture my hand-drawn study materials, my in-person study group sessions, or the hours I spent watching downloaded videos offline. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite failing the course, I actually learned a lot and grew as a student—I just couldn't demonstrate that knowledge on exams. My resilience is low, but I kept showing up, and that matters.
